topics.title: Super Cappuccino Crested Geckos: Scam or Legitimate Morph?

evidence.snippet: Don't buy super cappuccino crested gecko it's a scam and waste of money
Should We Breed Super Cappuccino Crested Geckos? — TikisGeckos explores the ethical and genetic debate
Why So Much Hate for Cappuccino Geckos? The Truth No One Wants to Say…

angleIdeas: Breeders call super cappuccino 'a scam'—but the incomplete-dominant genetics say it should be viable. Here's what the community is actually afraid of.
Two cappuccino geckos bred together don't always produce super cappuccinos. Most buyers assume they will. Here's why that matters for your breeding plan.
The cappuccino controversy isn't really about the morph. It's about whether breeders should be chasing homozygous animals at all.
Super cappuccino exists genetically, but breeding for it has become taboo. Here's the real cost—financial and ethical—of trying.

angleIdeas: Breeders often assume that pairing two cappuccinos will yield viable super cappuccinos at a predictable rate—the genetics tell a different story.
The 'scam' narrative around super cappuccinos isn't just hype: here's what the incomplete-dominant inheritance actually produces.
If you're planning a cappuccino breeding project, you need to understand why super cappuccino projects fail or succeed based on lineage, not just color.

Lilly whites carry the incomplete-dominant trait—here's how to spot the difference before you pair them with your breeding stock.
The belly pattern and edge coloration tell you whether you have an elite breeding animal or a visual morph without the genetic goods.

If you want to keep your line pure axanthic or cross strategically, you need to know the hidden genes your breeding stock is hiding.

Cappuccino × non-morph pairings consistently outperform Cappuccino × Cappuccino on hatchling vigor—here's the genetics and why.

angleIdeas: Soft Scale is incomplete dominant—breed two hets and you might expect 25% double-scaled, but most breeders don't understand what a Super Soft Scale should actually look like, so they're keeping and selling normals as 'het'.
The rarest outcome from Soft Scale × Soft Scale isn't failure—it's misidentification; here's how to spot true Super Soft Scales in your clutches.
Soft Scale breeding projects stall because breeders confuse incomplete dominance with co-dominance; the genetics are simple once you see it.
Google is asking about 'soft scale crested gecko' but breeder forums show confusion about progeny—a clear gap you can fill.

You bought two Cappuccinos expecting to breed Cappuccinos. Instead you got Frappuccinos. Here's the incomplete-dominant genetics behind the surprise.
Cappuccino isn't as simple as it looks: pairing two heterozygotes creates a Mendelian ratio that trips up new breeders.
The Frappuccino reveal: how a single pairing breaks down into three phenotypes and why that matters for your breeding plan.

When you buy a "black crested gecko," you're often paying premium prices for what will hatch as ordinary patterns. A breeder's guide to the genetics actually at play.
Dark and blue crested geckos are trending fast, but they're not recessive morphs—they're polygenic overlays. Understanding the difference saves thousands in failed pairings.

angleIdeas: Breeders think Frappuccino × Cappuccino will produce 'enhanced' Cappuccino—here's what genetics actually say.
Frappuccino is incomplete-dominant on top of another gene; pairing two Frappuccinos together doesn't guarantee the look you want.
If you're breeding Cappuccino for specific traits, a Frappuccino mate might cost you generations of selection.
The Frappuccino trend creates false expectations: most breeders don't understand what they're actually pairing.

topics.title: Extreme Harlequin Crested Geckos: Separating Hype from Reality

evidence.snippet: Google Trends rising query (related to "crested gecko", last 90 days US): "extreme harlequin crested gecko" +70%
Google autocomplete for "crested gecko" suggests "crested gecko colors" +60%

angleIdeas: Extreme Harlequin is polygenic—you can't guarantee it from any single pairing, and breeders overselling 'guaranteed extremes' are misleading.
Rising Extreme Harlequin searches suggest demand, but most buyers don't understand the breeding complexity behind the look.
If you're breeding Harlequin, chasing Extreme means selecting over multiple generations; buyers should know the real timeline.
YouTube 'rarest morph' hype drives demand but obscures the work: extreme harlequins are outcomes, not reliable genetics.
